data.adaptation_notes: H614 is a popular hybrid in the Kenyan highlands, with high production potential and high resistance to pest attacks. It requires more heat units to attain anthesis and physiological maturity than H612. Yield is maximized with early sowing (early March or early May) in AEZ II and AEZ III. The cultivar performed better than H612 across all AEZs, with a reduced likelihood of yield decline. Optimal management includes 75 kg N ha−1 fertilizer and recommended plant density. The cultivar is suitable for rainfed cropping systems and can increase maize production by up to 5.7% in the study area. However, the increment is possible if the nutrient management aligns with the government-recommended nitrogen fertilization of 75 kg N ha−1 (Chebet et al., 2017). Soil variables at five depths (0–20, 20–40, 40–60, 60–80, and 80–100 cm) were entered into the SBuild module in DSSAT. The experimental fields were optimally managed, and necessary measures, including weed control, fertilizer application, and pest and disease control, were applied based on standard agronomic practices. cross hybrids in the East Africa region, the model calibration results indicated that H614 require more heat units to attain anthesis and reach physiological maturity.

data.adaptation_notes: H612 showed a high probability of low production compared to H614, with >90% chance of yield below 5000 kg/ha in all AEZs. Its production was not significantly different across AEZs. Sowing in mid-March demonstrated high yield potential for H612 in all AEZs, but overall, H614 outperformed H612. H612 was originally released in Kenya and its DSSAT coefficients were calibrated and evaluated in the southern highlands of Tanzania, a region with similar climate. 2.7 Model application and spatial assessment 2.7.1 Evaluation of agronomic practices The study used the parameterized DSSAT-CERES-Maize model to evaluate various agronomic practices, namely, adjusting sowing dates (SDs) and cultivars, incorporating organic amendments with the recommended nitrogen fertilizer levels and tillage practices, and supplementing rainfed agriculture with varying levels of irrigation. Soil variables at five depths (0–20, 20–40, 40–60, 60–80, and 80–100 cm) were entered into the SBuild module in DSSAT. The experimental fields were optimally managed, and necessary measures, including weed control, fertilizer application, and pest and disease control, were applied based on standard agronomic practices. In addition to the calibrated coefficients from the on-farm experiments, the study also assessed the H612 cultivar, for maturity.

data.adaptation_notes: DEKA_LB is a long-duration maize cultivar recommended for low potential AEZs (LM3, LM4, LM5) in Embu county, Kenya. It is able to maintain yield levels under climate change scenarios, making it suitable for adaptation to increased temperatures. Optimal management includes early planting within the window, plant population of 40–50, and fertilizer nitrogen application of 60–80 kg/ha depending on AEZ and season. DSSAT and APSIM simulations indicate that early planting increases yields by 25% in SR season and 7% in LR season. High moisture stress is a major yield limiting factor in LM4 and LM5. Adaptation strategy for different agro-ecological zones with best combination of planting time, plant population, variety and fertilizer nitrogen for LR and SR seasons. Impact of climate change on the performance of different maize varieties under cultivation in different AEZs in Embu county of Kenya. This could be attributed to various factors such as differences in interpreting and translating farmer description of the resource endowment into model parameters, inability of the models to capture the effects of biotic stresses such as pests, diseases and weeds, inaccuracies in estimating per hectare yields from bags per plot as reported by farmers and inaccuracies in defining the initial conditions. This included, dates of sowing, days to tasselling and flowering, days to maturity and grain and dry matter yields at harvest.

location_contexts.evidence: This study examines the current and projected influence of climate change on maize yields in two major maize-producing counties of Kenya. Utilizing the calibrated and evaluated DSSAT-CERES-Maize model for the H614 maize cultivar, we investigated the projected impact of climate change on maize production with reference to a baseline period (1984–2013). Simulations were conducted for the mid-century period (2041–2070) and end-of-century period (2071–2100) using projected climate data from regional climate models under two RCP scenarios.

data.adaptation_notes: H614 is one of the oldest cultivars and is popular among farmers, especially in high-altitude regions. The model showed that H614 needed fewer heat units to attain juvenile and physiological maturity than KH600-23A. The H614 cultivar showed modest yield decrease under low nitrogen levels (N1 and N2) and performed better in the western region under high nitrogen. The eastern region showed a consistent downward trend in yield for H614 under high nitrogen. Sowing date and water management interactions were not significant for H614. The cultivar is stable and well adopted by farmers in the region. The contribution of the main effect of nitrogen application levels on the maize yields was also significant. 5) and soil water content were the variables with complete data at all sites and were therefore used for the evaluation (Figures S3-S5 in the supplementary file). The statistics showed that farmers could improve production by 52% if cultivar KH600-23A was planted in mid-February under nitrogen fertilization of 75 kg ­ha−1 with equal top dressing amount compared to the latest sowing date, with low nitrogen level and the slightly low yielding H614 cultivar. The calibration results from the present study showed that the KH600-23A cultivar needed more heat units to attain the juvenile and physiological maturity stages than the H614 cultivar.

The cultivar is recommended for adoption in Trans Nzoia County, especially under improved soil quality and structure. Yield trends are positive in northern and southern parts, but negative in eastern, western, and northern parts under poor soil conditions. The contribution of the main effect of nitrogen application levels on the maize yields was also significant. 5) and soil water content were the variables with complete data at all sites and were therefore used for the evaluation (Figures S3-S5 in the supplementary file). The statistics showed that farmers could improve production by 52% if cultivar KH600-23A was planted in mid-February under nitrogen fertilization of 75 kg ­ha−1 with equal top dressing amount compared to the latest sowing date, with low nitrogen level and the slightly low yielding H614 cultivar. The calibration results from the present study showed that the KH600-23A cultivar needed more heat units to attain the juvenile and physiological maturity stages than the H614 cultivar.

data.adaptation_notes: Katumani is located in a semi-arid zone in Kenya and is expected to experience a maize production decline of -25% to -30% under an extreme temperature rise of +3°C and a 30% decline in precipitation. Increased precipitation can compensate for yield losses from elevated temperatures, with a potential yield gain of 16% under increased precipitation and warming. Site-specific soil, water, and land management adaptation strategies are recommended, including soil and water conservation for dry regions and varying sowing dates for semi-humid to humid zones. Nutrient enhancement and cultivar variation are also suggested as feasible adaptation strategies. For the nitrogen fertilization application, the recom­ mended amounts in the published studies were adopted. Maize Yield in Different Sites Under the Baseline Period Maize yield was stimulated for the study sites under the synthetic climate change scenarios. The results show that warm­ ing accompanied by a decrease in precipitation might favour the highland areas, whereas the drought-prone regions might be severely affected. The calibration process determines the cultivar coefficients that govern the accumulation of temperature heat units and plant development from emergence to physiological matu­ rity (Jones et al., 2003).

data.adaptation_notes: H612 showed a high probability of low production compared to H614, with >90% chance of yield below 5000 kg/ha in all AEZs. Its production was not significantly different across AEZs. Sowing in mid-March demonstrated high yield potential for H612 in all AEZs, but overall, H614 outperformed H612. H612 was originally released in Kenya and its DSSAT coefficients were calibrated and evaluated in the southern highlands of Tanzania, a region with similar climate. 2.7 Model application and spatial assessment 2.7.1 Evaluation of agronomic practices The study used the parameterized DSSAT-CERES-Maize model to evaluate various agronomic practices, namely, adjusting sowing dates (SDs) and cultivars, incorporating organic amendments with the recommended nitrogen fertilizer levels and tillage practices, and supplementing rainfed agriculture with varying levels of irrigation. Soil variables at five depths (0–20, 20–40, 40–60, 60–80, and 80–100 cm) were entered into the SBuild module in DSSAT. The experimental fields were optimally managed, and necessary measures, including weed control, fertilizer application, and pest and disease control, were applied based on standard agronomic practices. In addition to the calibrated coefficients from the on-farm experiments, the study also assessed the H612 cultivar, for maturity.

data.adaptation_notes: DEKA_LB is a long-duration maize cultivar recommended for low potential AEZs (LM3, LM4, LM5) in Embu county, Kenya. It is able to maintain yield levels under climate change scenarios, making it suitable for adaptation to increased temperatures. Optimal management includes early planting within the window, plant population of 40–50, and fertilizer nitrogen application of 60–80 kg/ha depending on AEZ and season. DSSAT and APSIM simulations indicate that early planting increases yields by 25% in SR season and 7% in LR season. High moisture stress is a major yield limiting factor in LM4 and LM5. Adaptation strategy for different agro-ecological zones with best combination of planting time, plant population, variety and fertilizer nitrogen for LR and SR seasons. Impact of climate change on the performance of different maize varieties under cultivation in different AEZs in Embu county of Kenya. This could be attributed to various factors such as differences in interpreting and translating farmer description of the resource endowment into model parameters, inability of the models to capture the effects of biotic stresses such as pests, diseases and weeds, inaccuracies in estimating per hectare yields from bags per plot as reported by farmers and inaccuracies in defining the initial conditions. This included, dates of sowing, days to tasselling and flowering, days to maturity and grain and dry matter yields at harvest.

data.adaptation_notes: KH600-23A is a relatively new cultivar with high yield potential, out-performing older cultivars. It requires more heat units to reach maturity, and shows stability in yields under optimum nitrogen levels. Early sowing and high nitrogen fertilization (75-100 kg/ha) significantly enhance yields. The cultivar is recommended for adoption in Trans Nzoia County, especially under improved soil quality and structure. Yield trends are positive in northern and southern parts, but negative in eastern, western, and northern parts under poor soil conditions. The contribution of the main effect of nitrogen application levels on the maize yields was also significant. 5) and soil water content were the variables with complete data at all sites and were therefore used for the evaluation (Figures S3-S5 in the supplementary file). The statistics showed that farmers could improve production by 52% if cultivar KH600-23A was planted in mid-February under nitrogen fertilization of 75 kg ­ha−1 with equal top dressing amount compared to the latest sowing date, with low nitrogen level and the slightly low yielding H614 cultivar. The calibration results from the present study showed that the KH600-23A cultivar needed more heat units to attain the juvenile and physiological maturity stages than the H614 cultivar.

The cultivars that were grown in the fields were KH600-23A and H614. KH600-23A is a white, semiflint, late-maturing cultivar owned by the Agricultural Development Corporation (ADC) and first released in 2008. The two cultivars were selected mainly because of their high yield potential, resistance to pests and diseases, suitability to local climatic conditions, and wide adoption by farmers in the region.

data.adaptation_notes: Katumani is located in a semi-arid zone in Kenya and is expected to experience a maize production decline of -25% to -30% under an extreme temperature rise of +3°C and a 30% decline in precipitation. Increased precipitation can compensate for yield losses from elevated temperatures, with a potential yield gain of 16% under increased precipitation and warming. Site-specific soil, water, and land management adaptation strategies are recommended, including soil and water conservation for dry regions and varying sowing dates for semi-humid to humid zones. Nutrient enhancement and cultivar variation are also suggested as feasible adaptation strategies. For the nitrogen fertilization application, the recom­ mended amounts in the published studies were adopted. Maize Yield in Different Sites Under the Baseline Period Maize yield was stimulated for the study sites under the synthetic climate change scenarios. The results show that warm­ ing accompanied by a decrease in precipitation might favour the highland areas, whereas the drought-prone regions might be severely affected. The calibration process determines the cultivar coefficients that govern the accumulation of temperature heat units and plant development from emergence to physiological matu­ rity (Jones et al., 2003).

location_context.evidence: The experimental data for calibrating and evaluating the DSSAT–CERES-Maize model were sourced from 42 pure-stand maize fields in various parts of the county. The cultivars that were grown in the fields were KH600-23A and H614. Sowing varied across the fields between early March and late March. The management strategies assessment demonstrated significant feedback on sowing dates, nitrogen fertilization, and cultivars on maize yield. The eastern part of Trans Nzoia County demonstrated a consistent downwards trend for the vital yield enhancement strategies. Medium to high nitrogen levels revealed positive yield trends for more extensive coverage of the study area.

location_context.evidence: The experimental data for calibrating and evaluating the DSSAT–CERES-Maize model were sourced from 42 pure-stand maize fields in various parts of the county. The cultivars that were grown in the fields were KH600-23A and H614. KH600-23A is a white, semiflint, late-maturing cultivar owned by the Agricultural Development Corporation (ADC) and first released in 2008. The two cultivars were selected mainly because of their high yield potential, resistance to pests and diseases, suitability to local climatic conditions, and wide adoption by farmers in the region.

location_context.location_name: Trans Nzoia and Uasin Gishu counties
location_context.management_context: Simulated with DSSAT-CERES-Maize for H614; climate scenarios RCP4.5 and RCP8.5; main sowing window March–May
location_context.evidence: This study examines the current and projected influence of climate change on maize yields in two major maize-producing counties of Kenya. Utilizing the calibrated and evaluated DSSAT-CERES-Maize model for the H614 maize cultivar, we investigated the projected impact of climate change on maize production with reference to a baseline period (1984–2013). Simulations were conducted for the mid-century period (2041–2070) and end-of-century period (2071–2100) using projected climate data from regional climate models under two RCP scenarios.

data.adaptation_notes: H612 showed a high probability of low production compared to H614, with >90% chance of yield below 5000 kg/ha in all AEZs. Its production was not significantly different across AEZs. Sowing in mid-March demonstrated high yield potential for H612 in all AEZs, but overall, H614 outperformed H612. H612 was originally released in Kenya and its DSSAT coefficients were calibrated and evaluated in the southern highlands of Tanzania, a region with similar climate. 2.7 Model application and spatial assessment 2.7.1 Evaluation of agronomic practices The study used the parameterized DSSAT-CERES-Maize model to evaluate various agronomic practices, namely, adjusting sowing dates (SDs) and cultivars, incorporating organic amendments with the recommended nitrogen fertilizer levels and tillage practices, and supplementing rainfed agriculture with varying levels of irrigation. Soil variables at five depths (0–20, 20–40, 40–60, 60–80, and 80–100 cm) were entered into the SBuild module in DSSAT. The experimental fields were optimally managed, and necessary measures, including weed control, fertilizer application, and pest and disease control, were applied based on standard agronomic practices. In addition to the calibrated coefficients from the on-farm experiments, the study also assessed the H612 cultivar, for maturity.

data.adaptation_notes: H614 is a popular hybrid in the Kenyan highlands, with high production potential and high resistance to pest attacks. It requires more heat units to attain anthesis and physiological maturity than H612. Yield is maximized with early sowing (early March or early May) in AEZ II and AEZ III. The cultivar performed better than H612 across all AEZs, with a reduced likelihood of yield decline. Optimal management includes 75 kg N ha−1 fertilizer and recommended plant density. The cultivar is suitable for rainfed cropping systems and can increase maize production by up to 5.7% in the study area. However, the increment is possible if the nutrient management aligns with the government-recommended nitrogen fertilization of 75 kg N ha−1 (Chebet et al., 2017). data.adaptation_notes: DEKA_LB is a long-duration maize cultivar recommended for low potential AEZs (LM3, LM4, LM5) in Embu county, Kenya. It is able to maintain yield levels under climate change scenarios, making it suitable for adaptation to increased temperatures. Optimal management includes early planting within the window, plant population of 40–50, and fertilizer nitrogen application of 60–80 kg/ha depending on AEZ and season. DSSAT and APSIM simulations indicate that early planting increases yields by 25% in SR season and 7% in LR season. High moisture stress is a major yield limiting factor in LM4 and LM5. Adaptation strategy for different agro-ecological zones with best combination of planting time, plant population, variety and fertilizer nitrogen for LR and SR seasons. Impact of climate change on the performance of different maize varieties under cultivation in different AEZs in Embu county of Kenya. This could be attributed to various factors such as differences in interpreting and translating farmer description of the resource endowment into model parameters, inability of the models to capture the effects of biotic stresses such as pests, diseases and weeds, inaccuracies in estimating per hectare yields from bags per plot as reported by farmers and inaccuracies in defining the initial conditions. This included, dates of sowing, days to tasselling and flowering, days to maturity and grain and dry matter yields at harvest.

data.adaptation_notes: DK41 is represented as Deka_lb in the DSSAT model. It is recommended for LM3 and LM4 AEZs, with optimal planting in mid-March for LR season and mid-October (LM3) or late October (LM4) for SR season. Planting density of 50,000 plants/ha is optimal in these AEZs. Fertilizer nitrogen recommendations are 80 kg/ha (LM3) and 60 kg/ha (LM4). DK41 (Deka_lb) is considered a long-duration variety suitable for replacing short-duration varieties under climate change. 3.2.7 Impacts of soil fertility and nitrogen application. location_contexts.management_context: Simulated with DSSAT-CERES-Maize for H614; climate scenarios RCP4.5 and RCP8.5; main sowing window March–May
location_contexts.evidence: This study examines the current and projected influence of climate change on maize yields in two major maize-producing counties of Kenya. Utilizing the calibrated and evaluated DSSAT-CERES-Maize model for the H614 maize cultivar, we investigated the projected impact of climate change on maize production with reference to a baseline period (1984–2013). Simulations were conducted for the mid-century period (2041–2070) and end-of-century period (2071–2100) using projected climate data from regional climate models under two RCP scenarios.

location_contexts.management_context: Adaptation spots under RCP4.5; decreased yields projected
location_contexts.evidence: Adaptation spots under RCP4.5 were located mostly in the southern regions of Uasin Gishu County.

location_contexts.management_context: Adaptation spots under RCP4.5; decreased yields projected
location_contexts.evidence: Some eastern parts of Trans Nzoia County revealed decreased yields in the mid-century period.

location_contexts.management_context: Hotspots under RCP8.5; significant low-yield zones projected
location_contexts.evidence: The RCP8.5 scenario, however, revealed a slight difference in the representation of adaptation spots in Trans Nzoia County. Some northern parts of the county were mapped as hotspots.

data.adaptation_notes: Yield declines projected for mid-century (−7% to −20%) and end-of-century (−22% to −41%) periods, mainly due to increased temperature and suppressed precipitation during main sowing window (March–May). Adaptation spots identified in southern Uasin Gishu and eastern Trans Nzoia; hotspots in northern Trans Nzoia. Agronomic, vegetative, and soil/water management measures recommended to bridge yield gaps. The model outputs include water and nitrogen balance, leaf area index, biomass and grain yield (Liu et al. location_contexts.evidence: This study examines the current and projected influence of climate change on maize yields in two major maize-producing counties of Kenya. Utilizing the calibrated and evaluated DSSAT-CERES-Maize model for the H614 maize cultivar, we investigated the projected impact of climate change on maize production with reference to a baseline period (1984–2013). Simulations were conducted for the mid-century period (2041–2070) and end-of-century period (2071–2100) using projected climate data from regional climate models under two RCP scenarios.

location_contexts.evidence: The experimental data for calibrating and evaluating the DSSAT–CERES-Maize model were sourced from 42 pure-stand maize fields in various parts of the county. The cultivars that were grown in the fields were KH600-23A and H614. Sowing varied across the fields between early March and late March. The management strategies assessment demonstrated significant feedback on sowing dates, nitrogen fertilization, and cultivars on maize yield. Medium to high nitrogen levels revealed positive yield trends for more extensive coverage of the study area.

data.adaptation_notes: H614 is one of the oldest cultivars and is popular among farmers, especially in high-altitude regions. The model showed that H614 needed fewer heat units to attain juvenile and physiological maturity than KH600-23A. The H614 cultivar showed modest yield decrease under low nitrogen levels (N1 and N2) and performed better in the western region under high nitrogen. The eastern region showed a consistent downward trend in yield for H614 under high nitrogen. Sowing date and water management interactions were not significant for H614. The cultivar is stable and well adopted by farmers in the region. The contribution of the main effect of nitrogen application levels on the maize yields was also significant. 5) and soil water content were the variables with complete data at all sites and were therefore used for the evaluation (Figures S3-S5 in the supplementary file). The statistics showed that farmers could improve production by 52% if cultivar KH600-23A was planted in mid-February under nitrogen fertilization of 75 kg ­ha−1 with equal top dressing amount compared to the latest sowing date, with low nitrogen level and the slightly low yielding H614 cultivar. The calibration results from the present study showed that the KH600-23A cultivar needed more heat units to attain the juvenile and physiological maturity stages than the H614 cultivar.

data.adaptation_notes: Katumani is located in a semi-arid zone in Kenya and is expected to experience a maize production decline of -25% to -30% under an extreme temperature rise of +3°C and a 30% decline in precipitation. Increased precipitation can compensate for yield losses from elevated temperatures, with a potential yield gain of 16% under increased precipitation and warming. Site-specific soil, water, and land management adaptation strategies are recommended, including soil and water conservation for dry regions and varying sowing dates for semi-humid to humid zones. Nutrient enhancement and cultivar variation are also suggested as feasible adaptation strategies. For the nitrogen fertilization application, the recom­ mended amounts in the published studies were adopted. Maize Yield in Different Sites Under the Baseline Period Maize yield was stimulated for the study sites under the synthetic climate change scenarios. The results show that warm­ ing accompanied by a decrease in precipitation might favour the highland areas, whereas the drought-prone regions might be severely affected. The calibration process determines the cultivar coefficients that govern the accumulation of temperature heat units and plant development from emergence to physiological matu­ rity (Jones et al., 2003).
